question_title: Will California’s official statewide snowpack on April 1, 2026 be at least 80.0% of the April 1 average?
summary: California's statewide snowpack is at ~37% of April 1 average as of March 20, 2026, and needs to reach 80% — a 43 percentage point gap requiring the snowpack to more than double in 10 days. Instead of growing, the snowpack is actively melting at ~1.2% per day due to a record-breaking heat wave with temperatures 20-30°F above average. The NWS 7-day forecast shows zero snow or storms for the Sierra Nevada. NOAA forecasts above-normal temperatures continuing through spring. Climate scientist Daniel Swain says April 1 snowpack is likely to be the "worst ever observed by a considerable margin." At the current melt rate, snowpack will be ~25% by April 1. Even the most extreme historical "Miracle March" storms have added at most 20-30 percentage points — insufficient to close a 43-point gap — and no such storm is forecast. This is a near-impossibility.

factors.description: Current snowpack at ~37% of April 1 average (March 20 data from state climatologist), threshold is 80% — a 43 percentage point gap requiring more than doubling in 10 days
Snowpack declining at ~1.2% per day due to record heat wave; projected to reach ~25% by April 1 at current melt rate
NWS 7-day forecast shows NO snow or storms for Sierra Nevada — clear and warm conditions through end of March
Record-breaking heat dome over western US with temperatures 20-30°F above average; NOAA forecasts above-normal temps through spring
Climate scientist Daniel Swain says April 1 snowpack likely to be 'worst ever observed by a considerable margin'
Residual possibility of unprecedented late-March atmospheric river, but nothing forecast and heat would rapidly melt snow at lower elevations

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.01 (1% probability of YES)**

**Key findings:**

- **Current state**: Snowpack is at ~37% of April 1 average (as of March 20), needing 80% — a 43 percentage point gap
- **Trend**: Actively melting at ~1.2%/day; projected to reach ~25% by April 1
- **Weather**: Record heat dome with temps 20-30°F above normal; NWS shows zero storms in 7-day forecast; NOAA forecasts above-normal temps through spring
- **Expert view**: UC climate scientist Daniel Swain says this is likely to be the "worst April 1 snowpack ever observed by a considerable margin"
- **Historical context**: Even the most extreme "Miracle March" events added 20-30 percentage points — far less than the 43+ points needed while snow is actively melting

The snowpack would need to more than double in 10 days with no storms forecast and rapid ongoing melt. This is physically near-impossible.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will California’s official statewide snowpack on April 1, 2026 be at least 80.0% of the April 1 average?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42798)

[California’s Snowpack Shrinking Rapidly, Raising Concerns for Summer Water Supply - edhat](https://www.edhat.com/california/news/californias-snowpack-shrinking-rapidly-raising-concerns-for-summer-water-supply/)
[California's snowpack Is vanishing fast — and summer hasn't even started](https://calmatters.org/environment/water/2026/03/california-heat-wave-snow-reservoirs/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the California Department of Water Resources reports the statewide snowpack on April 1, 2026 at 80.0% or more of the April 1 average, using SnowTrax at https://snow.water.ca.gov/. It resolves to no otherwise.
fine_print: Use the statewide “Percent of April 1st average” figure for April 1, 2026. If the SnowTrax homepage is unavailable, use DWR’s snow surveys page. Candidate Sources: California DWR Snow Surveys https://water.ca.gov/Programs/Flood-Management/Flood-Data/Snow-Surveys
